ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken wing food product class; no specific entry for Drums of Heaven (spiced Indo-Chinese fried chicken wings). Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Drums of Heaven. A00LC (Chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for Drums of Heaven fried chicken wings. SNOMED CT coverage for prepared ethnic dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Drums of Heaven is 6 chicken drumette pieces, approximately 300 g total, as commonly served at Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken wings, cooked, fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05307 / FDC ID 171477)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked fried chicken wings used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values reflect deep-fried chicken wing meat with skin, which closely matches the Drums of Heaven preparation. Indian spice marinade (yogurt, chilli, garam masala) contributes negligible macronutrient change at typical usage levels; sodium is adjusted upward to reflect spiced marinade contribution. This is the closest approved-source match available; no HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry for Drums of Heaven was found.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Drums of Heaven. GI is estimated at 5 based on the analogous category of plain cooked chicken (GI ~0) and fried chicken with minimal breading. Fried chicken without significant starch coating has a very low GI due to negligible glycemic carbohydrate content (carbohydrate_g ~1.8 per 100g). GL per 300g serving = (5 × 5.4g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.27, rounded to 0.3. Classified as low GI per University of Sydney GI Database convention (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spiced marinade and deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Each 300 g serving of Drums of Heaven provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and reduced-sodium marinade preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Drums of Heaven has a very low glycemic index (estimated GI ~5) and negligible glycemic load per serving (~0.3), making it a low glycemic-impact choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though the high fat content from deep-frying warrants attention to overall caloric intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and cholesterol from deep-fried chicken skin
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 300 g serving provides approximately 16 g saturated fat and 285 mg cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and consider grilled or baked preparation alternatives to reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Drums of Heaven is a popular Indo-Chinese fusion appetiser consisting of chicken drumettes (the meatier section of the chicken wing) that are marinated in a spiced yogurt-based mixture containing ginger-garlic paste, red chilli, cumin, coriander, garam masala, turmeric, and lemon juice, then deep-fried until crispy and golden. The dish is widely served at Indian restaurants across Singapore and India, often as a starter or snack. The exterior is crispy and intensely spiced, while the interior remains juicy and tender. The flavour profile is bold, smoky, and mildly to moderately spicy, with aromatic notes from the whole spice blend. Nutritionally, Drums of Heaven is a high-protein, high-fat food due to the chicken meat and skin combined with deep-frying in oil. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 290 kcal, 27 g protein, and 19.5 g fat. The carbohydrate content is very low (~1.8 g per 100 g), resulting in a negligible glycemic load, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. However, the sodium content is elevated due to the spiced marinade, and the saturated fat content is clinically relevant for cardiovascular risk management. A standard serving of 6 pieces (~300 g) is a substantial protein source but should be consumed in moderation by individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Drums of Heaven 6 pieces?
Is Drums of Heaven suitable for diabetics?
What spices are used in Drums of Heaven?
Drums of Heaven vs buffalo wings
Indian spiced fried chicken wings Singapore

confidence_reason: No direct HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry for Drums of Heaven was found. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken wing data (FDC ID 171477), which is the closest approved-source match. Sodium is adjusted upward to reflect spiced marinade. GI is estimated by analogy from low-carbohydrate fried chicken data in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171477 (chicken wings, cooked, fried). No exact entry for Drums of Heaven exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN databases as of the access date. 2. Sodium_mg (420 mg per 100g) is adjusted upward from the USDA base value to account for the spiced yogurt marinade typical of Drums of Heaven preparation. 3. GI is estimated at 5 based on analogous fried chicken without significant starch coating; this is consistent with the very low carbohydrate content of the dish. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all micronutrients are reported from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171477 and are considered reliable estimates for this preparation. 5. The dish is classified as halal-compatible given standard chicken and spice ingredients, but halal certification status at specific restaurants should be verified independently. 6. is_gluten_free is set to true as the standard preparation uses no wheat-based coating; restaurant-specific preparations may vary. 7. Serving size of 300 g (6 pieces) is estimated based on typical drumette weight of approximately 50 g per piece as reported in USDA portion data.
